skill.name: ai-avatar-video
skill.description: Guides users through creating AI avatar, talking head, lipsync, and dubbing videos with the inference.sh belt CLI.
skill.summary: Create AI avatar and talking head video workflows with inference.sh model commands.
skill.author: infsh-skills

security_audit.summary: Static command-execution findings are mostly Markdown code examples and inline command references, not hidden executable code. The skill legitimately depends on the belt CLI and remote inference.sh services, so publication is acceptable with a privacy and external-service warning.

medium_findings.title: External CLI Commands Invoke Remote AI Services
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to run belt app commands for avatar, TTS, transcription, and lipsync workflows. This is expected for the skill, but it can submit prompts, image URLs, audio URLs, and video URLs to external inference services.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and central to the skill workflow. They appear legitimate, but they create real privacy and cost considerations because user media and scripts may be processed remotely.

medium_findings.title: Network Dependency on External Media and Documentation URLs
medium_findings.description: The skill contains inference.sh links and placeholder media URLs used as command inputs. The URLs are not evidence of exfiltration, but users should know that remote resources and hosted media are part of the workflow.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network references are visible documentation links and model inputs. I found no hidden endpoint or credential transfer, but external service use is confirmed.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Matches Are Static Analysis False Positives
low_findings.description: The analyzer reported Ruby or shell backtick execution, but the cited locations are Markdown code fences, inline code, or command examples. No evidence found of executable Ruby backtick syntax in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is Markdown and the suspicious characters delimit examples. The semantic context does not show hidden script execution beyond user-visible CLI instructions.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Finding Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged weak cryptography at the description line, but that line describes avatar video capabilities. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or other cryptographic use in the reviewed skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line is metadata prose, not code. I did not find cryptographic operations or configuration in the only scanned file.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bash Commands for External Model Runs
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill uses belt app run commands to execute model workflows through an external CLI. This is intended behavior, but it should be treated as an external command and network boundary.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command pattern is directly present in examples. It is not malicious by itself, but it can process user-controlled media and text through remote services.

content.value_statement: Teams need fast ways to turn scripts, portraits, and audio into presenter videos. This skill provides practical inference.sh CLI workflows for avatars, lipsync, dubbing, and related media steps.
actual_capabilities: Provides belt CLI examples for P-Video-Avatar text-to-avatar generation.
Shows audio-driven avatar commands for P-Video-Avatar, OmniHuman, Fabric, and PixVerse.
Compares listed avatar models by speed, cost, use case, and TTS support.
Documents multi-step workflows for portrait generation, TTS, transcription, translation, and lipsync.
Points users to related inference.sh skills and video app documentation.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh belt CLI and a working account login.
Remote model runs may upload scripts, image URLs, audio URLs, or video URLs to external services.
The skill provides workflow guidance and commands, not a local video generation engine.
Output quality depends on source media, selected model, and service availability.

use_cases.title: Product Demo Presenter
use_cases.description: Create a presenter video from a portrait and a short product script for launch or sales material.

use_cases.title: Course Explainer Avatar
use_cases.description: Generate talking head lessons from instructional scripts and consistent presenter images.

use_cases.title: Localized Video Dubbing
use_cases.description: Transcribe, translate, regenerate speech, and lipsync an existing video for another language.

prompt_templates.title: Beginner Avatar Video
prompt_templates.scenario: Create a simple presenter clip from a portrait and script.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use ai-avatar-video to help me create a short AI presenter video. I have a portrait URL and a 60-word script.

prompt_templates.title: Choose the Right Model
prompt_templates.scenario: Compare avatar video models before running a job.
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare the available avatar video models for my use case. I need clear speech, low cost, and a vertical social video.

prompt_templates.title: Audio-Driven Lipsync
prompt_templates.scenario: Use an existing voice recording with an avatar image.
prompt_templates.prompt: Build an audio-driven avatar workflow. I have a face image URL and a clean narration audio URL.

prompt_templates.title: Dubbing Pipeline
prompt_templates.scenario: Plan a multi-step localization workflow for an existing video.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a dubbing workflow for my existing video. Include transcription, translation handoff, new speech generation, and lipsync steps.

output_examples.input: A user asks for a low-cost avatar video from a portrait and launch announcement script.
output_examples.output: The assistant recommends P-Video-Avatar, lists the required portrait and script inputs, and explains the expected remote model run.

output_examples.input: A user has an existing narration file and wants the avatar mouth to match the audio.
output_examples.output: The assistant selects an audio-driven workflow and explains which image and audio URLs are needed before running the model.

output_examples.input: A localization producer wants to dub a short training video into another language.
output_examples.output: The assistant outlines transcription, translation, speech generation, and lipsync stages with privacy checks for the source media.

best_practices: Use clear, front-facing portraits with good lighting and simple backgrounds.
Review privacy and consent before sending faces, voices, or scripts to remote services.
Start with short clips to check quality, voice fit, timing, and cost before longer runs.
anti_patterns: Do not upload private faces, voices, or videos without permission from the people involved.
Do not assume placeholder URLs are valid production inputs.
Do not run long generation jobs before testing the selected model on a short sample.

faq.question: Does this skill generate video locally?
faq.answer: No. It guides use of the inference.sh belt CLI, which runs models through external services.

faq.question: Which model does the skill recommend first?
faq.answer: The skill recommends P-Video-Avatar first because the file describes it as fast, lower cost, and TTS-enabled.

faq.question: Can I use my own audio recording?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes audio-driven workflows that use an image URL and an audio URL.

faq.question: Can this help with multilingual dubbing?
faq.answer: Yes. It outlines transcription, translation, new speech generation, and lipsync steps for dubbing workflows.

faq.question: What should I prepare before using it?
faq.answer: Prepare approved portrait, audio, video, or script assets and confirm they can be processed by remote services.

faq.question: Is this safe for confidential media?
faq.answer: Use caution. The workflow can send media URLs and scripts to external services, so review data policies first.
